retrieved_docs.title: Neurobiology of Addiction -- Issues of Concern -- Withdrawal/Negative Affect Stage
retrieved_docs.content: The brain has a buffer for the "anti-reward" system, which includes cannabinoid, nociceptive, and neuropeptide Y neurotransmission. [14] [15] [16] [17] Adjustments in this buffering system may also lead to an increased propensity for addiction. Brain-imaging studies have revealed a decreased density of cannabinoid 1 (CB1) receptors in patients with alcohol use disorder. [18] The clinical consequences of an upregulated "anti-reward" system will present as irritability, anxiety, and dysphoria. The strengthened "anti-reward" system drives chronic withdrawal in patients with addictive disorders.

retrieved_docs.content: Glucose : Liver disease due to alcoholism may reduce glycogen stores, and ethanol impairs gluconeogenesis. As a consequence, patients with alcohol withdrawal develop anxiety, agitation, tremors, seizures, and diaphoresis, all of which can also occur with hypoglycemia.

retrieved_docs.title: Non-Dieting Approaches to Treatment of Obesity -- Issues of Concern -- 4. Naltrexone + Bupropion
retrieved_docs.content: Mechanism of action: Naltrexone=opioid antagonist: prevents beta-endorphin mediated negative feedback on alpha MSH + bupropion=inhibitor of DA and NE transporter: Stimulates POMC with increased release of alpha-MSH Year of FDA approval: 2014 Important trials: COR-I, COR-II, COR-BMOD, COR-Diabetes, LIGHT Approximate weight loss in % total body weight: 3.3 to 4.8 Dosing: Sustained release combination: 8-90 mg daily, up titrated to 16mg-180mg twice daily Contraindications: Pregnancy, seizure disorder, anorexia or bulimia, alcohol withdrawal, age >75 years, use of monoamine oxidase inhibitors within 2 weeks of drug initiation Precautions: Monitor for suicidal ideation, increase in heart rate Adverse Events: Nausea, vomiting, dizziness, dry mouth Important facts: Recommended for patients with obesity and a concurrent mood disorder. [8] [1] [3] [7] [11] [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Neurobiology of Addiction -- Issues of Concern -- Withdrawal/Negative Affect Stage
retrieved_docs.content: The desire to remove the negative feelings accompanying withdrawal primes the individual to further intoxication/binge via negative reinforcement. A vicious cycle ensues; taking the substance to lessen withdrawal will lead to worse withdrawal symptoms in the next period of abstinence.

retrieved_docs.title: Neurobiology of Addiction -- Issues of Concern -- Withdrawal/Negative Affect Stage
retrieved_docs.content: The second neuroadaptation in the withdrawal/negative affect stage results from the increased recruitment of stress circuits in the brain. This neuroadaptation is a between-systems process that includes the extended amygdala, often called the "anti-reward" system. [4] The predominant structures in this circuit are the bed nucleus of the stria terminalis (BNST), the central nucleus of the amygdala (CeA), and the shell of the NAcc. The upregulated anti-reward system leads to increased release of stress mediators such as dynorphin, corticotropin-releasing factor (CRF), norepinephrine (NE), orexin, and positive modulation of the hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal (HPA) axis.

retrieved_docs.title: Withdrawal Syndromes -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Alcohol Alcohol intoxication and withdrawal are complex. Most effects can be explained by the interaction of alcohol with neurotransmitters and neuroreceptors, including gamma-aminobutyric acid (GABA) and glutamate. [10] [11] The changes in the inhibitory and excitation neurotransmitters disrupt the neurochemical balance in the brain, causing symptoms of withdrawal. Ethanol inhibits opioid binding to mu-opioid receptors and long-term use results in the upregulation of opioid receptors. [12] [13] Opioid receptors in the nucleus accumbens and the ventral tegmental area of the brain modulate ethanol-induced dopamine release; this, in turn, produces alcohol craving and the use of opioid antagonists to prevent this craving. [12] [14] The "kindling" hypothesis of ethanol withdrawal describes the event where repeat and subsequent withdrawal in an individual becomes progressive and more severe. [15]

retrieved_docs.title: Neurobiology of Addiction -- Clinical Significance -- Addictions Neuroclinical Assessment
retrieved_docs.content: The negative emotionality domain correlates with the withdrawal/negative affect stage experienced by individuals with addictive disorders. The central behavior in this domain is negative reinforcement, often described as self-medication. [43] This domain is distinguished by increased anxiety and reduced hedonic tone, which clinically manifests as decreased pleasure in non–substance-related activities such as work and family. Symptoms in this domain are helped by medications targeting neurotransmitters imbalanced in withdrawal states, such as benzodiazepines.

retrieved_docs.title: Withdrawal Syndromes -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Differential diagnoses of withdrawal syndromes include: Acute coronary syndromes Acute hypoglycemia Addison disease Adrenal crisis Amphetamine toxicity Anticholinergic toxicity Anxiety disorder Central nervous disorder Cocaine toxicity Delirium tremens Dementia Depression Suicide Diabetic ketoacidosis Pancreatitis Hallucinogen toxicity Hyperthyroidism and thyroid storm Hyperventilation syndrome 3,4-methylenedioxymethamphetamine toxicity Panic disorder Phencyclidine toxicity Salicylate toxicity Status epilepticus Wernicke encephalopathy

retrieved_docs.title: Withdrawal Syndromes -- Complications -- Cocaine and stimulants
retrieved_docs.content: Although without a true withdrawal syndrome, complications of abstinence from stimulants include anxiety, anhedonia, and depression. Loss of productivity is possible due to fatigue and hypersomnia. A protracted withdrawal syndrome, sometimes termed the extinction phase, with the manifestation of behavior disturbances, including depression with suicidal thoughts, may also occur.

retrieved_docs.title: Withdrawal Syndromes -- Etiology -- Opioids
retrieved_docs.content: Both opioid and alpha-adrenergic receptors have the same effect on the potassium channel in the locus ceruleus. The medication clonidine has similar clinical findings in withdrawal, and this cross-tolerance explains why this medication can be used for the treatment of opioid withdrawal. Dexmedetomidine is a popular sedative utilized in the intensive care unit and is similar to clonidine as an alpha-adrenergic agonist but with more sedating properties; its withdrawal is similar to that of clonidine, with an unopposed excitatory effect.

retrieved_docs.title: Withdrawal Syndromes -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify patients who may be experiencing a withdrawal syndrome based on their clinical history and presentation. Differentiate between withdrawal syndromes associated with different substances, such as alcohol, opioids, and benzodiazepines. Implement appropriate treatment protocols, including symptom management and gradual tapering strategies, to safely manage withdrawal syndromes. Apply interprofessional team strategies to improve care coordination and outcomes in patients with withdrawal syndromes. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Mu Receptors -- Issues of Concern -- Tolerance and Physical Dependence
retrieved_docs.content: Signs of physiological disturbance usually accompany withdrawal syndrome. In contrast, the onset and duration of clinical manifestations, including pain (eg, abdominal cramping, bone pain, diffuse muscle aching), autonomic symptoms (eg, diarrhea, rhinorrhea, diaphoresis, lacrimation, shivering, nausea, emesis, piloerection, CNS arousal, sleeplessness, restlessness, tremors), and craving for the medication depend on the drug used. The addiction phenomenon is a potential consequence of drug dependence and is characterized by psychological and behavioral symptoms accompanied by drug craving, compulsive use, and a strong tendency to relapse after withdrawal.

retrieved_docs.content: Recurrent drinking resulting in failure to fulfill role obligations Recurrent drinking in hazardous situations Continued drinking despite alcohol-related social or interpersonal problems Evidence of tolerance Evidence of alcohol withdrawal or use of alcohol for relief or avoidance of withdrawal Drinking in larger amounts or over longer periods than intended Persistent desire or unsuccessful attempts to stop or reduce drinking A great deal of time spent obtaining, using, or recovering from alcohol Important activities are given up or reduced because of drinking Continued drinking despite knowledge of physical or psychological problems caused by alcohol Alcohol Craving Disorder Severity : The severity of alcohol use disorder at the time of diagnosis can be specified as a subtype based on the number of symptoms present: Mild: Two to 3 symptoms Moderate: Four to 5 symptoms Severe: Six or more symptoms

retrieved_docs.title: Withdrawal Syndromes -- Complications -- Alcohol
retrieved_docs.content: Complications of alcohol withdrawal include anxiety, depression, sleep disturbances, and hallucinations with disturbances of perception. More serious complications include seizures, delirium tremens, Wernicke-Korsakoff syndrome, and death. Although not a true complication of withdrawal, the Wernicke-Korsakoff syndrome is a nutritional deficiency of thiamine that often coincides with alcohol use and withdrawal; this syndrome is characterized by confusion with delirium, gait disturbances with ataxia, and paralysis of the eye muscles, termed ophthalmoplegia. Long-term memory impairment can occur in up to 80% of patients with alcohol use disorder who experience this syndrome. [71] [72] A protracted withdrawal syndrome can occur, with symptoms of anhedonia, irritability, insomnia, and craving, which occur past the expected course of standard withdrawal and potentially can be a reason for relapse. [73]

retrieved_docs.title: Drug and Alcohol Use (Archived) -- Etiology -- Genetic Factors
retrieved_docs.content: Among type 1 and type 2 alcoholics (typologies developed by Cloninger), type 1 alcoholics are characterized by the late onset of problem drinking, the rapid development of behavioral tolerance to alcohol, prominent guilt and anxiety related to drinking, and infrequent fighting and arrests when drinking. Cloninger also termed this subtype “milieu-limited,” which emphasizes the etiologic role of environmental factors. In contrast, type 2 alcoholics are characterized by early onset of an inability to abstain from alcohol, frequent fighting and arrests when drinking, and the absence of guilt and fear concerning drinking. Cloninger postulated that transmission of alcoholism in type 2 alcoholics was from fathers to sons, hence term the male-limited alcoholism. Type 1 alcoholics are characterized by high-reward dependence, high-harm avoidance, and low-novelty seeking. In contrast, type 2 alcoholics are characterized by high-novelty seeking, low-harm avoidance, and low-reward dependence. Cloninger also hypothesized that specific neurotransmitter systems underlie personality structure. Specifically, dopamine is hypothesized to modulate novelty seeking, characterized by frequent exploratory behavior and intensely pleasurable responses to novel stimuli. Serotonin is hypothesized to modulate harm avoidance, which is a tendency to respond intensely to aversive stimuli and their conditioned signals. Finally, norepinephrine is hypothesized to modulate reward dependence or the resistance to extinction of previously rewarded behavior.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2217
retrieved_docs.content: Psychoactive substances are the causative agents of psychoactive substance withdrawal, meaning that when the use of a psychoactive substance is reduced or stopped, individuals may experience a withdrawal syndrome characterized by a range of physical and psychological symptoms. Recognizing that psychoactive substances precipitate withdrawal is essential for clinicians to anticipate, prevent, and manage these symptoms, thereby improving patient safety and supporting successful cessation or tapering strategies.

retrieved_docs.content: Bupropion hydrobromide and bupropion hydrochloride are both contraindicated for use in patients experiencing withdrawal syndrome (متلازمة الانسحاب), indicating that these antidepressant formulations should be avoided in this clinical context. In contrast, tofisopam may be employed as a therapeutic option to treat withdrawal syndrome, suggesting a potential role for this anxiolytic agent in managing the symptoms associated with substance discontinuation.

Opioid‑related psychiatric and behavioral disorders, including opioid withdrawal states, are connected to the broader substance‑related disorder category. Poisoning and its sequelae form a parallel branch. “Disorder due to and following poisoning” is classified as a type of poisoning (التسمم) and specifically as poisoning by non‑medicinal substances, illustrating that toxic exposures beyond therapeutic drugs are included. This branch links alcoholic poisoning (التسمم الكحولي), drug‑induced levo‑fibrinogen degradation poisoning (تسمم بالأدوية المؤشرة على انحلال الليفين), and adrenal syndrome (متلازمة الأدرينالية). The category “Poisonings and Drug Effects” is positioned under both poisoning and drug dependence, reflecting the overlap between toxic exposures and substance‑related health effects. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 1828
retrieved_docs.content: Alcohol-related disorders encompass a range of health problems caused by excessive alcohol consumption. In the context of these disorders, beta‑D‑glucopyranose is contraindicated, indicating that this compound should not be administered to patients with alcohol‑related conditions due to potential adverse effects or lack of safety. Conversely, clorazepate, a benzodiazepine medication, may be employed as a therapeutic option for alcohol‑related disorders, often to manage withdrawal symptoms, anxiety, or related neuropsychiatric manifestations. Recognizing the contraindication of beta‑D‑glucopyranose and the potential utility of clorazepate is essential for safe and effective patient care in individuals affected by alcohol‑related disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 3 -- Community 1513
retrieved_docs.content: Alcohol-related disorders are medical conditions linked to excessive alcohol consumption and are clinically associated with a confusional state (حالة ارتباكية) as well as having a relationship with the term “الهَلْوَسة.” Pharmacologic management of alcohol-related disorders may include benzodiazepines such as oxazepam, clorazepate, and prazepam, which are indicated for treating withdrawal symptoms and related anxiety. Conversely, beta‑D‑glucopyranose is contraindicated in patients with alcohol-related disorders, indicating that this substance should be avoided in this population. Recognizing the association with confusional states and the contraindication of beta‑D‑glucopyranose helps clinicians provide comprehensive care while selecting appropriate therapeutic agents.

Cocaine withdrawal, SSRI withdrawal and hallucinogen dependence are classified as subtypes of psychoactive‑substance withdrawal. Withdrawal from SSRIs contraindicates the use of bupropion (hydrobromide or hydrochloride), whereas tofisopam may be employed to treat withdrawal syndrome. Hallucinogen dependence is linked to “halucinogeny”, which is also related to cholinergic antagonists; the combination product chlordiazepoxide / clidinium (linked to chlordiazepoxide hydrochloride) is classified as a cholinergic antagonist. Collectively, these interrelated classifications provide clinicians with a structured framework for therapeutic positioning, recognition of cross‑class effects, management of misuse and withdrawal phenomena, and selection of appropriate

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2144
retrieved_docs.content: Clonidine hydrochloride is a pharmacologic agent that can be employed in the management of disorders associated with opioid use, such as opioid withdrawal symptoms and other opioid‑related conditions. By targeting the underlying physiological mechanisms of these opioid‑related disorders, clonidine hydrochloride helps alleviate withdrawal effects and supports patient stabilization during opioid cessation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 1262
retrieved_docs.content: Bupropion, including its hydrobromide and hydrochloride salt forms, is contraindicated in patients who are experiencing متلازمة الانسحاب (withdrawal syndrome), indicating that this antidepressant and smoking‑cessation medication should be avoided during the acute phase of substance withdrawal. In contrast, tofisopam may be employed as a therapeutic option to treat متلازمة الانسحاب, suggesting a potential role for this anxiolytic agent in managing withdrawal‑related symptoms. Recognizing the contraindication of bupropion and the possible benefit of tofisopam is important for clinicians when selecting safe and effective pharmacologic strategies for individuals undergoing withdrawal.

This hierarchy supports comprehensive diagnosis, risk assessment, and integrated patient care across the spectrum of substance use and addiction medicine.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 1 -- Community 207
retrieved_docs.content: Anti‑Addiction/Substance Abuse Treatment Agents form a broad pharmacologic class that includes specific subclasses for alcohol dependence and nicotine dependence. Within this class, Alcohol Deterrents/Anti‑craving agents are derived from naltrexone hydrochloride, which serves as the parent compound for treatments of opiate dependence and alcohol dependence and is also classified as an alcohol‑withdrawal agent; the combination product bupropion / naltrexone links antidepressant therapy with anti‑addiction pharmacology. Disulfiram belongs to drugs used in alcohol dependence, is additionally categorized among sulfur‑containing ectoparasiticides, and has a contraindicated class relationship with paraldehyd, indicating unsafe co‑administration. Citalopram, primarily an antidepressant, may be repurposed for alcohol‑dependence treatment, illustrating cross‑application of psychotropic agents in addiction care. Drugs used in nicotine dependence are identified as a subclass of addictive‑disorder
